content.body: The adaptive immune system provides specific protection against pathogens. It has two branches: humoral immunity (antibodies) and cell-mediated immunity (T-cells). It also provides immunological memory.

Innate immunity: fast, non-specific (neutrophils, macrophages)
Adaptive immunity: slow, specific, has memory

Humoral: B-cells → Plasma cells → Antibodies (IgG, IgM, IgA, IgE, IgD)
Cell-mediated: T-helper, T-cytotoxic, T-regulatory cells

Antibody structure: 2 heavy + 2 light chains, variable regions bind antigen

content.title: Primary vs Secondary Response
content.body:
Primary response: First exposure to antigen
- Slow (5-10 days), low antibody level, mainly IgM
- Memory B and T cells are created

Secondary response: Re-exposure
- Fast (1-3 days), high antibody level, mainly IgG
- This is why vaccines work — they prime the memory response

questions.question: Which cells produce antibodies?
questions.answer: Plasma cells (from B-cells)
questions.explanation: B-cells differentiate into plasma cells which secrete antibodies

questions.question: Immunological memory is carried by:
questions.answer: Memory B and T cells
questions.explanation: Long-lived memory cells enable faster, stronger response upon re-exposure

questions.question: The secondary immune response is faster because:
questions.answer: Memory cells are already primed
questions.explanation: Memory cells recognise the antigen quickly and mount a rapid response

questions.question: T-cytotoxic cells directly kill:
options: Virally infected cells and tumour cells
questions.answer: Virally infected cells and tumour cells
questions.explanation: Cytotoxic T-cells (CD8+) kill infected and cancerous cells directly

questions.question: Which antibody class is found in breast milk (colostrum)?
questions.answer: IgA
questions.explanation: Secretory IgA is the main antibody in mucous membranes and is passed in breast milk

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

content.body: The adaptive (specific) immune response provides long-lasting protection against specific pathogens. It has two arms: humoral immunity (antibody-mediated) and cell-mediated immunity (T lymphocyte-mediated).

content.title: Humoral Immunity
content.body:
B lymphocytes → recognize antigen → activated → divide (clonal selection)
→ Plasma cells (secrete antibodies) + Memory B cells

Antibodies (immunoglobulins):
• Y-shaped proteins with variable and constant regions
• Antigen-binding site specific to one antigen
Functions: neutralisation, opsonisation (coating for phagocytosis), complement activation, agglutination

content.title: Cell-Mediated Immunity
content.body:
T helper cells (CD4⁺): activated by antigen presented on MHC II (APCs)
→ Release cytokines (interleukins)
→ Stimulate B cells and cytotoxic T cells

Cytotoxic T cells (CD8⁺): kill infected cells presenting antigen on MHC I
→ Release perforin and granzymes
→ Target cell undergoes apoptosis

Important in: viral infections, transplant rejection, cancer surveillance

questions.question: Plasma cells are derived from...
questions.answer: B lymphocytes
questions.explanation: When activated by antigen, B lymphocytes differentiate into plasma cells (antibody secreting) and memory B cells.

questions.question: Antibodies are shaped like...
questions.answer: The letter Y
questions.explanation: Antibodies (immunoglobulins) have a Y-shaped structure with two heavy chains and two light chains, forming two antigen-binding sites.

questions.question: Opsonisation means...
options: Coating a pathogen with antibodies to enhance phagocytosis
questions.answer: Coating a pathogen with antibodies to enhance phagocytosis
questions.explanation: Opsonins (antibodies, complement) coat pathogens, making them easier for phagocytes to recognise and engulf.

questions.question: T helper cells (CD4⁺) are activated by antigen on...
questions.answer: MHC class II molecules
questions.explanation: Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) display pathogen peptides on MHC class II. CD4⁺ T cells recognise this complex.

questions.question: Cytotoxic T cells kill by releasing...
questions.answer: Perforin and granzymes
questions.explanation: Cytotoxic T cells release perforin (creates pores in target cell membrane) and granzymes (enzymes triggering apoptosis).

questions.question: Memory cells provide...
options: Faster stronger response to subsequent infection
questions.answer: Faster stronger response to subsequent infection
questions.explanation: Memory B and T cells persist after infection. Re-exposure triggers a faster, stronger secondary immune response.

questions.question: MHC class I molecules are found on...
questions.answer: All nucleated cells
questions.explanation: MHC class I presents peptides from inside the cell (e.g. viral proteins) on the surface of ALL nucleated cells.

questions.question: Vaccines work by...
options: Introducing antigens to stimulate memory cell production
questions.answer: Introducing antigens to stimulate memory cell production
questions.explanation: Vaccines expose the immune system to antigens (weakened/killed pathogens or their proteins) without causing disease, generating memory cells.

questions.question: HIV attacks which immune cells?
questions.answer: T helper cells (CD4⁺)
questions.explanation: HIV specifically infects and destroys CD4⁺ T helper cells, progressively destroying adaptive immunity (leading to AIDS).

questions.question: Monoclonal antibodies are produced from...
options: Fused B cell and myeloma cell (hybridoma)
questions.answer: Fused B cell and myeloma cell (hybridoma)
questions.explanation: Hybridoma technology: B cells (antibody-producing) fused with myeloma cells (immortal) → hybridomas that produce identical monoclonal antibodies indefinitely.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Autoimmune diseases occur when the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own tissues. Immunotherapy uses immune system principles to treat cancer and other diseases.

Self/non-self discrimination:
MHC (Major Histocompatibility Complex)/HLA proteins mark cells as 'self'
T cells undergo positive and negative selection in thymus
Negative selection: T cells recognising self-antigens eliminated (clonal deletion)
Failure → autoimmune disease

Autoimmune examples:
Type 1 diabetes: T cells destroy β cells of pancreas
Rheumatoid arthritis: antibodies against joint synovium
Multiple sclerosis: attack on myelin sheath
Lupus (SLE): antibodies against nuclear antigens

content.title: Immunotherapy
content.body:
Monoclonal antibodies: engineered antibodies targeting specific proteins
Herceptin (trastuzumab): targets HER2 on breast cancer cells
PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors: release 'brakes' on T cells to attack cancer
(e.g. pembrolizumab — used for melanoma, lung cancer)
CAR-T therapy: genetically engineer patient's T cells to recognise cancer antigens
Immune tolerance therapies for autoimmune disease

questions.question: Autoimmune diseases occur because
options: self-tolerance fails and immune cells attack body's own tissues
questions.answer: self-tolerance fails and immune cells attack body's own tissues
questions.explanation: Normally T cells that react to self-antigens are eliminated (clonal deletion). If this fails → autoimmunity

questions.question: Type 1 diabetes is autoimmune because
options: cytotoxic T cells destroy insulin-producing β cells of pancreas
sugar is toxic to immune system
questions.answer: cytotoxic T cells destroy insulin-producing β cells of pancreas
questions.explanation: T₁DM: autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β cells → no insulin → type 1 diabetes (requires insulin injections)

questions.question: MHC/HLA molecules mark cells as 'self' by
questions.answer: displaying self-peptides to T cells
questions.explanation: MHC presents peptide fragments on cell surface; T cells recognise self-MHC presenting self-peptides as normal (tolerised in thymus)

questions.question: Negative selection in thymus eliminates T cells that
options: strongly react to self-antigens (would cause autoimmunity)
questions.answer: strongly react to self-antigens (would cause autoimmunity)
questions.explanation: Clonal deletion: thymocytes reacting too strongly to self-peptides+MHC are apoptosed before leaving thymus → prevents autoimmunity

questions.question: Monoclonal antibodies used in cancer therapy work by
options: specifically binding cancer-cell surface proteins, blocking growth or flagging for immune destruction
questions.answer: specifically binding cancer-cell surface proteins, blocking growth or flagging for immune destruction
questions.explanation: mAbs like Herceptin bind HER2 (overexpressed in some breast cancers): blocks growth signalling, marks cell for ADCC killing

questions.question: PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors work by
options: blocking inhibitory signals that cancer uses to hide from T cells → T cells attack cancer
questions.answer: blocking inhibitory signals that cancer uses to hide from T cells → T cells attack cancer
questions.explanation: Tumours express PD-L1 → suppresses T cells via PD-1. Checkpoint inhibitors block this → T cells attack tumour

questions.question: CAR-T therapy stands for
options: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T cells — patient T cells engineered to recognise cancer
questions.answer: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T cells — patient T cells engineered to recognise cancer
questions.explanation: CAR-T: take patient T cells, genetically insert CAR recognising cancer antigen (e.g. CD19 for leukaemia), reinfuse → targeted cancer killing

questions.question: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is autoimmune because
options: immune cells attack myelin sheath of neurons, causing demyelination and neurological symptoms
questions.answer: immune cells attack myelin sheath of neurons, causing demyelination and neurological symptoms
questions.explanation: MS: autoreactive T cells cross blood-brain barrier and attack oligodendrocytes/myelin → demyelination → impaired nerve conduction

questions.question: Why are steroids (corticosteroids) used to treat autoimmune flares?
options: they broadly suppress immune activity, reducing autoimmune inflammation
questions.answer: they broadly suppress immune activity, reducing autoimmune inflammation
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids: suppress inflammation and immune cell activation broadly → reduce autoimmune damage. Long-term use has side effects

questions.question: Rheumatoid factor (RF) test detects antibodies against
options: IgG (self-antibodies — autoimmune marker for rheumatoid arthritis)
questions.answer: IgG (self-antibodies — autoimmune marker for rheumatoid arthritis)
questions.explanation: RF: IgM antibodies against Fc region of IgG. Present in ~70-80% RA patients — diagnostic marker of autoimmunity

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Blood groups (ABO system):
Type A: A antigens on RBC, anti-B antibodies in plasma
Type B: B antigens, anti-A antibodies
Type AB: both antigens, no antibodies (universal recipient)
Type O: no antigens, both anti-A and anti-B (universal donor)

Rhesus factor: Rh+ (D antigen present) or Rh- (absent)
Haemolytic disease of newborn (HDN): Rh- mother, Rh+ fetus

Organ transplantation: match MHC/HLA types
Rejection: host immune system attacks graft
Immunosuppressants: cyclosporin, tacrolimus

content.title: Blood Transfusion
content.body:
Mismatched transfusion:
1. Recipient's antibodies bind donor RBC antigens
2. Agglutination (clumping)
3. Complement activation → haemolysis
4. Haemolytic transfusion reaction (potentially fatal)

Type O Rh- is universal donor (no antigens)
Type AB Rh+ is universal recipient (no antibodies)

content.title: Transplant Rejection
content.body:
Hyperacute: within minutes (pre-existing antibodies to donor)
Acute: days-weeks (T cell mediated)
Chronic: months-years (gradual immune damage)
Graft-versus-host (GvHD): donor T cells attack host (bone marrow transplants)
HLA matching: closer match → less rejection risk

questions.question: Blood type O is universal donor because
options: type O RBCs have no A or B antigens — no antibodies triggered in any recipient
questions.answer: type O RBCs have no A or B antigens — no antibodies triggered in any recipient
questions.explanation: No antigens on Type O RBCs → recipient's anti-A and anti-B antibodies have nothing to bind → no reaction

questions.question: Blood type AB is universal recipient because
options: AB plasma contains no anti-A or anti-B antibodies — won't attack any donor RBCs
questions.answer: AB plasma contains no anti-A or anti-B antibodies — won't attack any donor RBCs
questions.explanation: Type AB: no antibodies in plasma → can receive A, B, AB or O red cells without antibody-mediated rejection

questions.question: Haemolytic disease of newborn (HDN) occurs when
options: Rh- mother sensitised in first pregnancy makes anti-Rh antibodies that cross placenta and destroy Rh+ fetal RBCs in subsequent pregnancy
questions.answer: Rh- mother sensitised in first pregnancy makes anti-Rh antibodies that cross placenta and destroy Rh+ fetal RBCs in subsequent pregnancy
questions.explanation: First pregnancy: Rh+ fetal cells enter Rh- mother at birth → she makes anti-D antibodies. Second Rh+ pregnancy: IgG anti-D crosses placenta → destroys fetal RBCs

questions.question: Agglutination in mismatched blood transfusion is caused by
options: antibodies crosslinking multiple RBCs bearing the target antigen
questions.answer: antibodies crosslinking multiple RBCs bearing the target antigen
questions.explanation: Each antibody has two binding sites → bridges between cells → clumping (agglutination) → blockage of vessels + haemolysis

questions.question: HLA matching improves transplant success because
options: closer match → less immune recognition of graft as foreign → less rejection
questions.answer: closer match → less immune recognition of graft as foreign → less rejection
questions.explanation: HLA/MHC molecules display cell-surface peptides. Similar HLA → T cells less likely to recognise graft cells as non-self

questions.question: Cyclosporin (immunosuppressant) works by
options: suppressing T cell activation (inhibits IL-2 production) to prevent rejection
questions.answer: suppressing T cell activation (inhibits IL-2 production) to prevent rejection
questions.explanation: Cyclosporin: inhibits calcineurin → blocks IL-2 gene transcription → T cells can't proliferate → less rejection. Side effect: increased infection risk

questions.question: Graft-versus-host disease (GvHD) differs from transplant rejection because
options: in GvHD, donor immune cells attack the recipient's tissues (reversed attack)
questions.answer: in GvHD, donor immune cells attack the recipient's tissues (reversed attack)
questions.explanation: GvHD: occurs in bone marrow transplants where donor T cells recognise recipient's tissues as foreign and attack them

questions.question: Anti-D injection (Rhogam) given to Rh- mothers works by
options: destroying Rh+ fetal cells in mother's blood before she can be sensitised, preventing formation of memory cells
questions.answer: destroying Rh+ fetal cells in mother's blood before she can be sensitised, preventing formation of memory cells
questions.explanation: Anti-D passive immunisation: anti-D antibodies destroy fetal Rh+ cells that entered maternal circulation before they can stimulate immune memory → prevents HDN in next pregnancy

questions.question: Type A patient receives Type B blood. What happens?
options: Anti-A antibodies react with B antigens — safe
Anti-B antibodies react with B antigens on donor RBCs → agglutination → haemolytic reaction
questions.answer: Anti-B antibodies react with B antigens on donor RBCs → agglutination → haemolytic reaction
questions.explanation: Type A patient has anti-B antibodies. Transfused Type B cells carry B antigens → antibody binding → agglutination and haemolysis

questions.question: Universal plasma donor blood type is
questions.answer: AB
questions.explanation: Type AB plasma has no anti-A or anti-B antibodies → can give plasma to any blood type without causing reaction (converse to O being universal RBC donor)

questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Immunology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Immunology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Immunology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Immunology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Immunology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Immunology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Immunology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Immunology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Immunology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Immunology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Immunology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Immunology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Immunology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Immunology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
